topic: Why people obey authority even when they should not — Milgram

script.title: Why You'd Obey Orders That Kill

scenes.narration: In 1961, a Yale psychologist ran an experiment that revealed something terrifying about ordinary people. The results shook the entire scientific world.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir style, an empty clinical room with a single metal chair under a harsh overhead light, long shadows on bare concrete floor, ominous atmosphere

scenes.narration: Stanley Milgram wanted to understand how Nazi soldiers followed orders. What he discovered had nothing to do with Germany — and everything to do with you.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir, a hand gripping a thick stack of official documents stamped with bold authority seals, shadows obscuring the hand's identity

scenes.narration: Volunteers were told to deliver electric shocks to strangers. Each wrong answer meant a higher voltage. Most assumed they would stop. They were wrong.
scenes.image_prompt: Film noir black and white, a close-up of a hand hovering over a row of numbered switches, knuckles tense, deep shadows cast across the panel

scenes.narration: When the 'subject' screamed in pain and begged to stop, participants hesitated — but sixty-five percent kept going. All the way to the maximum voltage.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white, a locked heavy door with light seeping beneath it, a silhouette of a figure pressed against the outside, chains coiled on the floor beside it

scenes.narration: They were not monsters. They were teachers, accountants, ordinary people. What broke their moral compass was one simple thing: an authority figure saying 'please continue.'
scenes.image_prompt: Film noir black and white, a cracked mirror reflecting a fragmented face distorted into multiple warped images, harsh overhead spotlight, deep shadow surroundings

scenes.narration: Milgram called this the 'agentic state' — the moment your brain shifts from independent agent to obedient instrument. You stop owning your actions. Someone else does.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white noir, a marionette puppet hanging limp from tangled strings in an empty dark room, strings extending upward into impenetrable darkness

scenes.narration: This same psychological switch explains corporate fraud, military atrocities, and abuse inside institutions. Ordinary people. Legitimate authority. Catastrophic outcomes.
scenes.image_prompt: Film noir black and white, a long empty corridor with identical closed doors on both sides receding into darkness, a single overhead bulb casting a narrow cone of light

scenes.narration: The most dangerous belief you carry is that you would have refused. Milgram's data says otherwise. The question isn't whether you can be manipulated — it's whether you know when it's happening.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir, a single empty chair in a vast dark void, a faint shadow of a figure cast on the floor in front of it with no visible source, deeply unsettling
